topic: Context 2: Local, National, International and Global Areas of Interest
subject: spanish

items.q: How do you say 'I am going to visit' using the near future?
items.correct: 'Voy a visitar' - ir conjugated, then a, then the infinitive.
items.why: The near future needs all three parts (ir + a + infinitive); dropping the 'a' is among the most frequent errors in writing about plans.

items.q: What does 'el campo' mean when describing where you live?
items.correct: The countryside (it can also mean a field or a sports pitch).
items.why: 'Campo' looks like 'camp', but a campsite is 'un camping'; the false friend muddles descriptions of rural versus urban life.

items.correct: A disadvantage - useful for weighing up life in your region: 'Una desventaja es que no hay transporte público'.
items.misconception: An advantage, ignoring the des- prefix.
items.why: The prefix des- reverses the meaning, as in English dis-; missing it flips the sense of for-and-against arguments completely.
The answer examiners want is: Homeless people. “People without cars” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: 'Se debería proteger el medio ambiente' means what?
items.why: It's The environment should be protected. “The environment was protected” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: Which sentence correctly says 'I am going to visit Madrid'?
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Voy a visitar Madrid. “Voy visitar Madrid” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.
The answer examiners want is: Rubbish. “The beach” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.
